title: Delivery Reliability
subtitle: C7 — Your code is yours from merge to deprecation
markdown:
## Why this matters

In a startup, the engineer who *shipped* the feature is the engineer who *fixes it at 2 AM* when it breaks. There is no separate "ops team" to throw it over to. This sounds harsh — it's actually the most leveraged thing about working at small companies.

Ownership compounds. The engineer who watches their own production traffic for a week learns more about the system than 10 engineers who only write code and walk away. By month 6, they're proposing the architecture changes that prevent the next 5 incidents.

This page is about the habits of people who own.

## The ownership spectrum

From least to most owned:

- **Level 0:** "I shipped the code. Not my problem if it breaks." (Junior contractor mindset.)
- **Level 1:** "I'll fix it if someone tags me." (Most junior engineers.)
- **Level 2:** "I have alerts on my code; I see issues before users report them." (The senior shift.)
- **Level 3:** "I track the metrics weekly; I'm proposing the next refactor before it becomes a problem." (Staff-engineer behaviour.)

Where you operate on this spectrum, more than your code quality, decides how fast you move from junior to senior.

## The four habits of owners

### 1. Add the alert before you ship
If your code can fail in production, instrument it. "It worked in dev" is not enough. The first time the new code page-faults at 3 AM, you find out — not the user, not the customer-success team.

### 2. Write the runbook
For any non-trivial feature, the team needs a one-pager: what does this do, where do logs live, what's the rollback procedure, who to call. If you ship without writing this, you're stealing time from your future self and every other on-call engineer.

### 3. Read your own logs the day after launch
Not "check that nothing's broken" — *actively read* the production log of the code you just shipped. You'll find at least three things you didn't anticipate. Patch them in the next PR.

### 4. Don't blame, RCA
When something breaks, the engineer who owns it does the post-mortem. Not "who is to blame" — *what conditions made this possible*, and *what change makes it impossible going forward*. Engineers who do this become the ones leadership trusts with bigger systems.

## The honest pattern in the industry

The engineers who get promoted fast in their first 3 years are not the ones who write the most code. They're the ones who **make their teams' systems more reliable while writing code at a normal pace.** Reliability is the cumulative effect of ownership habits. There's no shortcut.

The engineers who plateau are the ones who optimise for shipping volume and *avoid* on-call. They write a lot of code that someone else has to maintain. Eventually that someone else becomes the senior engineer.

diagrams.caption: The ownership ladder
diagrams.source:
graph BT
    L0[Level 0 Not my problem] --> L1[Level 1 Fix when tagged]
    L1 --> L2[Level 2 Alerts on my code]
    L2 --> L3[Level 3 Tracking metrics and proposing refactors]
    L3 --> Staff[Staff Engineer trajectory]
    style L0 fill:#fee2e2
    style L1 fill:#fde68a
    style L2 fill:#dbeafe
    style L3 fill:#dcfce7
    style Staff fill:#86efac

tutorOpener: Think about a feature you've shipped. What level of ownership do you have on it today? What would it take to move up one level?
